BigMart Sales Analysis

Ananya Jha

library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.2.1     ✔ readr     2.2.0
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.3     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.2     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(here)
## here() starts at /home/ananyalytics/Documents/R/bigmart-sales-analysis
library(ggplot2)
library(ggthemes)

1. Introduction

The goal of this project is to uncover business insights by analyzing retail sales from BigMart outlets and identifying the key factors influencing product sales.

The analysis focuses on:

  • Sales distribution patterns
  • Impact of pricing and visibility
  • Outlet performance comparison
  • Product category contribution

2. Data Overview

data <- read_csv(here("data", "cleaned", "bigmart_cleaned.csv"))
## Rows: 8523 Columns: 14
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (8): item_identifier, item_fat_content, item_type, outlet_identifier, ou...
## dbl (6): item_weight, item_visibility, item_mrp, outlet_establishment_year, ...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
glimpse(data)
## Rows: 8,523
## Columns: 14
## $ item_identifier           <chr> "FDA15", "DRC01", "FDN15", "FDX07", "NCD19",…
## $ item_weight               <dbl> 9.300, 5.920, 17.500, 19.200, 8.930, 10.395,…
## $ item_fat_content          <chr> "low fat", "regular", "low fat", "regular", …
## $ item_visibility           <dbl> 0.016047301, 0.019278216, 0.016760075, 0.022…
## $ item_type                 <chr> "Dairy", "Soft Drinks", "Meat", "Fruits and …
## $ item_mrp                  <dbl> 249.8092, 48.2692, 141.6180, 182.0950, 53.86…
## $ outlet_identifier         <chr> "OUT049", "OUT018", "OUT049", "OUT010", "OUT…
## $ outlet_establishment_year <dbl> 1999, 2009, 1999, 1998, 1987, 2009, 1987, 19…
## $ outlet_size               <chr> "Medium", "Medium", "Medium", "Unknown", "Hi…
## $ outlet_location_type      <chr> "Tier 1", "Tier 3", "Tier 1", "Tier 3", "Tie…
## $ outlet_type               <chr> "Supermarket Type1", "Supermarket Type2", "S…
## $ item_outlet_sales         <dbl> 3735.1380, 443.4228, 2097.2700, 732.3800, 99…
## $ outlet_age                <dbl> 27, 17, 27, 28, 39, 17, 39, 41, 24, 19, 27, …
## $ price_category            <chr> "Premium", "Low", "High", "High", "Low", "Lo…

The dataset contains over 8,500 observations with multiple features related to product characteristics, outlet details, and sales performance.

3. Data Cleaning Summary

The dataset required several preprocessing steps:

  • Missing values in item_weight were imputed using group mean

  • Missing outlet_size replaced with “Unknown”

  • Zero values in item_visibility were imputed using group median

  • Standardized item_fat_content categories

  • Converted multiple “character” data types into “factors”

  • Created new features:

    • outlet_age
    • price_category

Note: Exploratory Analysis Approach

This report presents a selection of key exploratory analyses and visualizations. The complete analysis, including detailed EDA and intermediate outputs, is available in the scripts/ directory.

The focus of this report is to highlight the most relevant insights and business-critical patterns derived from the data.

4. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

4.1 Univariate Analysis

summary(data$item_outlet_sales)

data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = item_outlet_sales)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "#8A226AFF", bins = 50) + 
  labs(
    title = "Sales Distribution",
    subtitle = "Majority of Products cluster in lower sales range, with a few high-performing outliers",
    x = "Item Outlet Sales",
    y = "Number of Products",
  ) + 
  theme_fivethirtyeight() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans"),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text()
  )

The sales variable shows a wide distribution with a right-skew, indicating that a small number of products generate significantly higher revenue compared to the majority.

4.2 Bivariate Analysis

Sales by Outlet Type

sales_by_outlet <- data %>%
  group_by(outlet_type) %>%
  summarise(avg_sales = mean(item_outlet_sales, na.rm = TRUE),
            total_sales = sum(item_outlet_sales)) %>%
  ungroup()

Different outlet types show significant variation in total sales, suggesting that store format plays a key role in performance.

Sales by Location

sales_by_location <- data %>%
  group_by(outlet_location_type) %>%
  summarise(avg_sales = mean(item_outlet_sales, na.rm = TRUE),
            total_sales = sum(item_outlet_sales)) %>%
  ungroup()

Sales patterns differ across location tiers, indicating geographical influence on revenue generation.

4.3 Multivariate Analysis

Outlet Age vs Sales

cor(data$outlet_age, data$item_outlet_sales)

The correlation between outlet age and sales is weak, suggesting that older outlets do not necessarily perform better than newer ones.

5. Visualizations

1. Price vs Sales

data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = item_mrp, y = item_outlet_sales)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#F9C932FF") + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#8A226AFF") + 
  labs(
    title = "Sales vs MRP",
    subtitle = "Higher item price points indicate positive correlation with total outlet revenue",
    x = "Item MRP",
    y = "Outlet Sales"
  ) +
  theme_fivethirtyeight() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans"),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text()
  )
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Higher-priced items tend to generate higher sales, indicating a positive correlation between price and revenue.

2. Visibility vs Sales

data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = item_visibility, y = item_outlet_sales)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#F98C0AFF") + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#BA3655FF") +
  labs(
    title = "Visibility vs Sales",
    subtitle = "High visibility items do not consistently outperform low visibility ones",
    x = "Visibility",
    y = "Outlet Sales"
  ) + 
  theme_fivethirtyeight() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans"),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text()
  )
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Item visibility does not show a strong or consistent impact on sales performance.

3. Outlet Age vs Sales

data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(outlet_age), y = item_outlet_sales)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#C44E52FF", alpha = 0.9) +
  labs(
    title = "Outlet Age vs Outlet Sales",
    subtitle = "Sales variability remains similar across outlet ages",
    x = "Outlet Age",
    y = "Outlet Sales"
  ) +
  theme_fivethirtyeight() + 
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans"),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text()
  )

Sales distribution appears relatively consistent across outlet ages, suggesting limited impact of outlet age on performance.

4. Sales by Outlet Type

sales_by_outlet <- read_csv(here("data", "processed", "sales_by_outlet.csv"))
## Rows: 4 Columns: 3
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (1): outlet_type
## dbl (2): avg_sales, total_sales
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
sales_by_outlet %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(outlet_type, total_sales), y = total_sales)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "#F98C0AFF") + 
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) + 
  labs(
    title = "Total Sales by Outlet Type",
    subtitle = "Supermarket Type 1 generates the most revenue, significantly outperforming other outlets",
    x = "Outlet Type",
    y = "Outlet Sales"
  ) + 
  theme_fivethirtyeight() + 
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans"),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text()
  )

Supermarket Type 1 generates the highest total sales, significantly outperforming other outlet types.

5. Sales by Location Tier

sales_by_location <- read_csv(here("data", "processed", "sales_by_location.csv"))
## Rows: 3 Columns: 3
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (1): outlet_location_type
## dbl (2): avg_sales, total_sales
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
sales_by_location %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = outlet_location_type, y = total_sales)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "#57106EFF") + 
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Sales by Location Type",
    subtitle = "Tier 3 locations appears to generate highest sales, suggesting a strong market presence in suburban or rural zones",
    x = "Location Type",
    y = "Total Sales"
  ) + 
  theme_fivethirtyeight() + 
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans"),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text(),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 30, 10, 10)
  )

Tier 3 locations generate the highest sales, indicating strong performance in non-urban markets.

6. Sales by Category

sales_by_category <- read_csv(here("data", "processed", "sales_by_category.csv"))
## Rows: 16 Columns: 3
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (1): item_type
## dbl (2): avg_sales, total_sales
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
sales_by_category %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(item_type, total_sales), y = total_sales)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "#BA3655FF") + 
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Sales by Category",
    subtitle = "Fruits & Vegetables and Snack Foods dominate total sales contribution",
    x = "Item Type",
    y = "Total Sales"
  ) + 
  theme_fivethirtyeight() + 
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans"),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text()
  )

Fruits & Vegetables and Snack Foods dominate total sales contribution.

7. Sales by Fat Content

sales_by_fat <- read_csv(here("data", "processed", "sales_by_fat.csv"))
## Rows: 2 Columns: 3
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (1): item_fat_content
## dbl (2): avg_sales, total_sales
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
sales_by_fat %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = item_fat_content, y = total_sales)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "#8A226AFF") + 
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) + 
  coord_flip() + 
  labs(
    title = "Sales by Fat Content",
    subtitle = "Low-fat products account for a larger portion of total sales compared to regular-fat alternatives",
    x = "Fat",
    y = "Sales"
  ) + 
  theme_fivethirtyeight() + 
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "sans"),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size =14),
    axis.text = element_text()
  )

Low-fat products contribute more to total sales than regular-fat products.

6. Key Insights

  • Sales are heavily skewed toward a small number of high-performing products
  • Higher MRP items tend to generate more revenue
  • Visibility has limited impact on sales performance
  • Supermarket Type 1 dominates total sales
  • Tier 3 locations outperform urban areas
  • Certain product categories contribute disproportionately to revenue

7. Business Recommendations

  • Focus inventory on high-performing product categories
  • Expand operations in Tier 3 locations
  • Optimize pricing strategies for premium products
  • Improve performance of underperforming outlet types

8. Conclusion

This analysis highlights key drivers of retail performance and provides actionable insights for improving sales strategy and operational efficiency across BigMart outlets.

````
